fingerprint: The 10.59 Hz 'raw theoretical' is Schumann's spherical formula f₁ = (c/2πR)√2 requiring Earth's radius. More importantly, all five measured Schumann harmonics follow the √(n(n+1)) spacing pattern — the eigenvalue signature of the Laplacian on a sphere (spherical harmonics / Legendre polynomials). A flat disc produces Bessel-function spacing that diverges from the measured pattern. The dome cannot explain the harmonic spacing.

globe_values.name: Spherical harmonic ratio f₂/f₁
globe_values.source: Eigenvalues of angular Laplacian on S²: n(n+1) for n=1,2,3...

globe_values.name: a = πR_Earth
globe_values.source: AE projection identity — same fingerprint as WIN-001 and Section 4.5.9

dome_values.name: Dome predicts via quarter-wave: f = c/(4H)
dome_values.value: H(r) averaged over disc gives 22–35 Hz, not 10.59 or 7.83
dome_values.note: Quarter-wave formula gives vertical standing waves, not circumferential Schumann modes

dome_values.name: Flat disc (cylindrical cavity) TM mode ratio f₂/f₁
dome_values.note: Bessel-zero spacing, systematically below measured ratios

harmonic_spacing_test.description: The Schumann harmonics are a smoking gun: their frequency ratios are diagnostic of cavity geometry. Spherical gives √(n(n+1))/√2, flat disc gives Bessel zero ratios. The measured harmonics match the sphere.

sphere_ratios.note: Ideal lossless sphere; measured ratios are slightly higher because ionospheric losses lower the Q-factor of the fundamental more than higher harmonics in absolute terms, but the PATTERN is spherical — ratios diverge from disc values in the correct direction

disc_ratios.note: Bessel-zero spacing for a cylindrical cavity of radius 20,015 km. Systematically too low and with wrong curvature — ratios grow much more slowly than measured

harmonic_spacing_test.verdict: Measured harmonic ratios cluster around spherical prediction (within 8–10% from Q-correction), and diverge dramatically from disc prediction (13–40% off). The five Schumann harmonics collectively fingerprint a spherical cavity.

why_it_matters: The dome model only discusses the Schumann fundamental (7.83 Hz) and can fudge a match by adopting the measured value. But there are FIVE Schumann harmonics, and their frequency ratios are determined by the cavity geometry. The √(n(n+1)) spacing is the unique signature of the Laplacian on a 2-sphere — it comes from spherical harmonics, the natural eigenfunctions of a spherical shell. On a flat disc, the eigenfunctions are Bessel functions with completely different zero-spacing. The measured harmonics (7.83, 14.1, 20.3, 26.4, 32.8 Hz) follow the spherical pattern, not the disc pattern. This is not a single-number coincidence — it's a five-point pattern match to spherical geometry. The dome's 'aetheric damping' fudge can scale a single frequency but cannot reshape the entire harmonic spectrum from disc-spacing to sphere-spacing.
algebraic_detail: On a sphere of radius a: the angular Laplacian has eigenvalues -n(n+1)/a² with eigenfunctions Y_nm (spherical harmonics). The resonant frequencies are f_n = (c/2πa)√(n(n+1)). On a flat disc of radius R with Dirichlet/Neumann boundary: the radial Laplacian has eigenvalues -(α_mn/R)² where α_mn are zeros of Bessel functions J_m. The resonant frequencies are f_mn = c·α_mn/(2πR). These two spectra have fundamentally different structure: √(n(n+1)) grows as ~n for large n (approximately equally spaced), while Bessel zeros α_mn grow as ~(m+2n-½)π/2 (different spacing law). The dome's 10.59 Hz = (c/2πa)√2 with a=R_Earth. Using the dome's a_dome=πR_Earth, this becomes c√2/(2·a_dome) — the π from the sphere cancels against the π embedded in a_dome, as identified in Section 4.5.9.
suggested_text: The Schumann resonance provides a five-point geometric fingerprint. The measured harmonics (7.83, 14.1, 20.3, 26.4, 32.8 Hz) follow the ratio pattern f_n/f_1 ≈ √(n(n+1)/2), which is the eigenvalue spectrum of the Laplacian on a sphere — the unique mathematical signature of spherical harmonics. A flat disc of the dome's dimensions produces Bessel-function eigenvalues with ratios (1.59, 2.14, 2.30, 2.65) that diverge from the measured pattern by 13–40%. The dome model discusses only the fundamental and never predicts the harmonics — because doing so would require spherical geometry.
note_on_Q_correction: The measured ratios (1.80, 2.59, 3.37, 4.19) exceed the ideal lossless sphere ratios (1.73, 2.45, 3.16, 3.87) by 4–8%. This is expected: the Earth-ionosphere cavity is lossy, with Q-factors of 4–6 for the fundamental decreasing for higher harmonics. Lossy resonator theory (complex eigenfrequencies) shifts the peak frequencies and alters ratios. Detailed numerical models using realistic ionospheric conductivity profiles (e.g., Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002) reproduce all five measured harmonics within 1–2%. The disc ratios, by contrast, are systematically TOO LOW and diverge in the wrong direction — no amount of damping correction can transform disc spacing into the measured pattern.
